title: Extracts from the First Version of Hardyng's Chronicle
summary: The repository XML is a pinned copy of University of Michigan CME item CME00119, which encodes C. L. Kingsford's October 1912 article and selective transcription of three discontiguous passages from John Hardyng's first-version Chronicle. Kingsford transcribed the sole first-version witness, British Library Lansdowne MS 204, rather than Ellis's 1812 later-version edition; Ellis supplies only limited comparison passages. The selection comprises the dedication and most of the prologue, praise of Henry V, and a composite conclusion, plus Kingsford's preface and tailnote. A complete Internet Archive scan and OCR of each Kingsford issue, a complete Ellis scan, the complete METS 2015 Volume I PDF, and DIMEV v1.0 are cached with exact work locators. The British Library's 474-canvas IIIF object is publicly readable but deliberately not cached because its item-level reuse link is dead and current provider terms do not establish permission for bulk recirculation.
review_notes: The primary subject is the checked-in XML artifact; the cataloged intellectual unit is Kingsford's edited selection, not the complete first-version Chronicle, manuscript, or journal issue.
Lansdowne MS 204 is the sole direct first-version witness. Ellis's 1812 later-version edition is cited only for passage-level comparisons and is not a base witness.
The three numbered XML units are substantive verse selections. Their embedded manuscript-derived arguments and rubrics are paratext, not extra numbered works.
The modern METS Volume I PDF contains the introduction, dedication, prologue, and Books 1–3; it does not contain the Book 7 extracts printed by Kingsford.
Public BL image delivery and permission to bulk-cache or redistribute those images are distinct questions; the absence of a cache is not an availability claim.

agents.name: Digital Index of Middle English Verse

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00119
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's conversion and publication workflow, supplied through the pinned Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: The file is 33,200 bytes; IDG/@ID, BIBNO, and VID all identify CME00119.
The root text declares LANG="eng enm" and the 2016 TEMPHEAD entry records MURP proofing and structural adjustment.
The XML has three numbered DIV2 selections, 78 seven-line LG elements, 546 L elements, 14 PB elements spanning printed pages 740–753, and 11 NOTE elements.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item CME00119
entities.description: A public structured digital text reproducing Kingsford's preface, three numbered selections, arguments and notes, and tailnote from the October 1912 article.
notes: The item page attributes the medieval text to Hardyng and the edition to Kingsford and applies No Copyright–United States.
The initial keying date, production source, and correction history before the documented 2016 review remain unresolved.

entities.label: Kingsford, Extracts from the First Version of Hardyng's Chronicle, 1912
entities.description: The immediate source edition: a selective transcription of three discontiguous passages from Lansdowne MS 204, with Kingsford's short preface, historical notes, damage restorations, limited later-version comparisons, and tailnote.

bibliographic.citation: C. L. Kingsford, ‘Extracts from the First Version of Hardyng's Chronicle,’ The English Historical Review 27, no. 108 (October 1912): 740–753, https://doi.org/10.1093/ehr/XXVII.CVIII.740.
bibliographic.title: Extracts from the First Version of Hardyng's Chronicle

notes: The article prints the introduction, praise of Henry V, and conclusion in full as Kingsford defined those three selections; it does not print the intervening Chronicle.
Kingsford's final locator says f. 226v, while current BL foliation and images place the envoy on f. 226r and the map on f. 226v.

entities.label: Kingsford, The First Version of Hardyng's Chronicle, 1912
entities.description: Kingsford's preceding analytical article on Lansdowne MS 204, explicitly referenced by the October extracts article as its first installment and explanatory context.

bibliographic.citation: C. L. Kingsford, ‘The First Version of Hardyng's Chronicle,’ The English Historical Review 27, no. 107 (July 1912): 462–482, https://doi.org/10.1093/ehr/XXVII.CVII.462.
bibliographic.title: The First Version of Hardyng's Chronicle

notes: This article analyzes the manuscript and promises the three extracts published in the next issue; it is contextual scholarship, not the immediate transcription source for the repository XML.

entities.label: British Library Lansdowne MS 204
entities.description: The sole surviving manuscript of Hardyng's first-version Chronicle and the direct base witness for Kingsford's selection.

identifiers.scheme: British Library Archives and Manuscripts record

date_statements.label: current British Library catalog date
date_statements.notes: The official catalog gives this object-production range.

date_statements.label: modern edition production context
date_statements.value: 1450s, probably as a presentation manuscript completed in 1457
date_statements.notes: This scholarly interpretation is preserved separately from the current catalog's 1440–1450 range.

physical_description.extent: 230 folios, with two unfoliated paper flyleaves at the beginning and two at the end
notes: Gothic cursive in at least two hands; Hand 1 writes ff. 5r–225v and marginalia, while Hand 2 writes f. 2v, ff. 3r–4r, f. 4v, ff. 226r–230v, headings, and other additions.
Middle English and Latin; produced in eastern England, probably Lincolnshire or East Anglia.
Contents include dedication f. 2v, prologue ff. 3r–4r, incomplete table f. 4v, Books 1–7 ff. 5r–230v, Umfraville arms f. 220r, map ff. 226v–227r, and Latin documents ff. 227v–230v.
The catalog calls this the sole copy of the first version and distinguishes at least sixteen later-version copies.
Current target loci are ff. 2v–4r, f. 216r final stanza through f. 217v first stanza, f. 220r final stanza through f. 223v first three stanzas, and f. 226r final two stanzas.

entities.label: British Library complete IIIF facsimile of Lansdowne MS 204
entities.description: A public IIIF Presentation 3 object of 474 canvases, from front cover through spine, including all folios and flyleaves.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation 3 manifest with JPEG image services
notes: The complete public object was audited but not bulk-cached; the access record documents the separate no-cache decision.
The target canvases are 10–13, 437–440, 445–452, and 457. Canvas 458 shows the map on f. 226v.

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of The English Historical Review 27, no. 108
entities.description: A complete 229-page issue scan, OCR text, and item metadata containing Kingsford's extracts article on PDF pages 120–133, corresponding to printed pages 740–753.
entities.reproduction_format: complete-issue PDF, OCR text, and metadata JSON

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of The English Historical Review 27, no. 107
entities.description: A complete 209-page issue scan, OCR text, and item metadata containing Kingsford's first article on PDF pages 46–66, corresponding to printed pages 462–482.
entities.reproduction_format: complete-issue PDF, OCR text, and metadata JSON

entities.label: Ellis, The Chronicle of Iohn Hardyng, 1812
entities.description: Henry Ellis's edition of Hardyng's later, second version, used by Kingsford only for limited comparison with two Henry V stanzas, the altered envoy, and the omitted Scotland itinerary.

bibliographic.citation: John Hardyng, The Chronicle of Iohn Hardyng, ed. Henry Ellis (London: printed for F. C. and J. Rivington et al., 1812).
bibliographic.title: The Chronicle of Iohn Hardyng

notes: Relevant comparison loci are printed p. 388, pp. 420–421, and pp. 423–429; these do not convert the later version into a second base witness for CME00119.

entities.label: Internet Archive Harvard–Google facsimile of Ellis's 1812 Hardyng
entities.description: A complete Google-derived scan supplied through Internet Archive, with OCR and scan pagination metadata.
entities.reproduction_format: complete-volume PDF, OCR text, and scandata XML

entities.label: Simpson and Peverley, John Hardyng's Chronicle, Volume I, 2015
entities.description: The first published volume of a modern edition from Lansdowne MS 204. It includes the scholarly introduction, dedication, prologue, table, and Books 1–3, but not the Book 7 praise and conclusion corresponding to most of Kingsford's extracts.

bibliographic.citation: John Hardyng, John Hardyng's Chronicle, Edited from British Library MS Lansdowne 204, vol. 1, ed. James Simpson and Sarah Peverley (Kalamazoo: Medieval Institute Publications, 2015).
bibliographic.title: John Hardyng's Chronicle, Edited from British Library MS Lansdowne 204, Volume I

notes: The edition recovers erased Prol.155–161 under ultraviolet light; Kingsford and CME00119 end the prologue at Prol.154.
The project-level description of a complete edition must not be read as proof that the currently available Volume I contains all seven books.

entities.label: DIMEV v1.0 record 1174 / IMEV 710
entities.description: A Chronicle-level verse record identifying Hardyng, seven-line ababbcc stanzas, twenty witness entries across both versions, and Lansdowne wit-1174-10 at ff. 5–226; it catalogs Kingsford pp. 740–753 as extracts only.
notes: The active record does not identify Kingsford's three selections as separate works; a dedication record appears only as commented-out source data.
DIMEV's bibliography pairs Kingsford's first-article title with pp. 740–753, but the article itself and OUP metadata establish the immediate article's exact Extracts title.

scope.notes: The repository artifact is a local/pinned representation of the U-M item; the exact export transaction date is unknown.

rights.component: fifteenth-century Hardyng text selected in CME00119
rights.basis: The medieval text was created in the fifteenth century and is far outside current U.S. copyright terms; this claim concerns the historical text, not modern editorial matter or images.

rights.component: 1912 published article, transcription, preface, and editorial notes
rights.basis: The article was published in 1912, before the current U.S. public-domain threshold for published works stated in Copyright Office Circular 15A.
notes: Current OUP access controls do not alter the U.S. copyright status of the 1912 article.

rights.component: University of Michigan CME digital text and associated public digital materials
rights.basis: The U-M item page applies the standardized No Copyright–United States statement to the digital materials.
notes: This provider statement is separate from the medieval text, Kingsford's article, repository license, and source-image rights.

rights.component: repository XML artifact and its modern markup
rights.basis: The pinned CME repository root LICENSE is the Unlicense but expressly describes software and does not identify XML data or this file's markup as covered. The license is therefore retained as evidence without broadening it to the artifact.
notes: The uncertainty concerns the modern digital artifact and markup, not the independently public-domain medieval text or 1912 article.
Do not infer an open data license solely from the software-worded root license.

rights.component: Internet Archive issue scan, OCR, and metadata derivatives
rights.basis: The underlying 1912 article is public domain in the United States, but the item metadata exposes no item-specific open license for the modern scan and OCR derivatives.
The article was published in 1912, before the current U.S. public-domain threshold for published works stated in Copyright Office Circular 15A.

rights.component: Internet Archive issue scan, OCR, and metadata derivatives
rights.basis: The underlying 1912 article is public domain in the United States, but the item metadata exposes no item-specific open license for the modern scan and OCR derivatives.

rights.component: 1812 printed edition, including Ellis's editorial text
rights.basis: The edition was published in 1812, long before the current U.S. public-domain threshold for published works.

rights.component: Google-derived scan, OCR, and pagination files
rights.basis: The underlying 1812 edition is public domain in the United States, but no item-specific open license for the modern Google scan derivatives was found in the Internet Archive metadata.

rights.component: existing British Library IIIF manuscript photographs and image services
rights.jurisdiction: British Library provider terms; reuse jurisdiction-dependent
rights.basis: The IIIF manifest exposes public images but points to an item-level manuscript-image reuse page that currently returns HTTP 404. Current general BL terms apply, but no clear open image license or bulk-cache permission was found.
notes: Public availability is recorded separately from the unresolved reuse position; the images were not bulk-cached.

rights.component: physical ownership, onsite consultation, and request administration
rights.basis: Ownership and access to the physical manuscript are not themselves copyright in the medieval text; consultation remains subject to institutional access conditions.

rights.component: newly supplied photography or reproduction files and permission to reproduce them
rights.jurisdiction: British Library imaging-service terms; reuse jurisdiction-dependent
rights.basis: Current imaging-service terms make supply and reuse subject to service conditions and written permission; any third-party rights remain separate.

rights.component: 2015 scholarly edition, editorial introduction, apparatus, and PDF/web presentation
rights.basis: METS states that digital editions are for individual use and directs requests to reprint to the project; public readability is not an open license for the modern edition.

rights.component: METS source XML files when separately supplied by the project
rights.basis: The METS copyright and citation page separately licenses its XML under Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International.
notes: This component claim does not relicense the cached edition PDF.

rights.component: DIMEV v1.0 source-data release, including record 1174 and bibliography
rights.basis: The tagged v1.0 archive includes a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International license.

editorial_practices.source_feature: A complete seven-book first-version Chronicle in the sole Lansdowne 204 witness.
editorial_practices.representation: Three discontiguous passages printed as Introduction, Praise of Henry V, and Conclusion, with an omitted Scotland chapter summarized rather than transcribed.
editorial_practices.description: Kingsford explicitly selects the three passages he considers most distinctive and presents them in article order. The resulting edition and CME00119 are not complete witnesses of Hardyng's Chronicle.
editorial_practices.scope: Kingsford pp. 740–753 and all medieval verse in CME00119
notes: The three numbered units are substantive selections. Their embedded descriptive arguments are manuscript-derived paratext, not extra numbered works.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Torn corner damage in three prologue stanzas and limited parallel passages in the same witness and later version.
editorial_practices.representation: Bracketed restorations partly from a parallel passage later in Lansdowne 204, plus notes citing Ellis for selected later-version parallels.
editorial_practices.description: Kingsford documents the local damage restoration and passage comparisons. Ellis is a comparison source only; there is no second first-version witness to collate.
Direct comparison with the images shows editorial punctuation, but Kingsford supplies no systematic statement of his punctuation policy in the article.

editorial_practices.representation: A reading text with modern editorial capitalization patterns.
editorial_practices.description: Direct comparison indicates editorial capitalization, but Kingsford supplies no systematic method statement; the degree of intervention is therefore recorded cautiously.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Kingsford's article hierarchy, page breaks, notes, arguments, quoted envoy, and prose framing.
editorial_practices.representation: Structured XML with preface, three numbered DIV2 selections, nested components, PB markers for pp. 740–753, notes, and a tailnote containing the envoy.
editorial_practices.description: The encoding closely preserves the article's selection boundary and hierarchy, including editorial prose, rather than reconstructing the complete manuscript work.
notes: The XML records a 2016 MURP proof and structural adjustment, but the original keying procedure is unknown.

editorial_practices.source_feature: The sole first-version witness and a seven-book Chronicle.
editorial_practices.representation: Volume I publishes the dedication, prologue, table, and Books 1–3 only.
editorial_practices.description: The available modern volume is a single-witness edition with explicit volume limits; it recovers erased Prol.155–161 that Kingsford did not print.

editorial_practices.representation: Manuscript spelling is mostly retained with minor changes and punctuation is supplied minimally for accessibility.
editorial_practices.description: The editors state an accuracy-and-accessibility policy, retain most manuscript spelling, make minor changes, use minimal punctuation, and document material and editorial features in notes.

open_questions.question: Does the CME submodule's software-worded Unlicense apply to CME00119 XML data and markup?
next_steps: Seek an explicit repository statement identifying whether XML data and markup are covered components.
Until clarified, keep the artifact status unknown and do not treat U-M's NoC-US statement as a worldwide open-data license.

open_questions.question: What initial keying source, date, transcription method, correction history, and bulk-source package preceded the repository XML's documented 2016 MURP review?
next_steps: Ask the University of Michigan Library for item-level production metadata and any preserved source package for CME00119.
Compare any supplied source with the October 1912 issue and checked-in XML without treating the 2016 review as the initial encoding date.

open_questions.question: Why does Kingsford locate the final two stanzas at f. 226v when current British Library foliation and images place them on f. 226r and the map on f. 226v?
next_steps: Ask the British Library whether the manuscript was refoliated or whether Kingsford's side designation is simply an error.
Retain both the historical printed locator and current verified folio/canvas in discovery data.

open_questions.question: What is the current publication status of the promised later METS volume containing Book 7 and the complete modern text corresponding to Kingsford's praise and conclusion extracts?
next_steps: Ask METS or the editors for the status of Volume II and whether a stable critical text or line mapping for Book 7 is available.
Do not imply that the cached 2015 Volume I contains the conclusion merely because the project description discusses a complete multivolume edition.

open_questions.question: What current item-level terms govern bulk caching and redistribution of the public Lansdowne MS 204 IIIF images now that the manifest's manuscript-image reuse link returns HTTP 404?
next_steps: Ask the British Library to identify the replacement item-level rights route and whether complete IIIF bulk caching and repository recirculation are permitted.
Keep the facsimile status publicly_available but omit local copies until permission or a clear open license is documented.
